did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models (specifically InceptionResNetV2 and MobileNetV3Large) combined with Soft-Attention mechanisms and a custom loss function to classify skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The task is dermatological skin lesion classification on imbalanced data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of deep learning models (InceptionResNetV2, MobileNetV3Large, etc.) with Soft-Attention mechanisms and a custom loss function for skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology, experimental results with metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC), and comparisons, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Lesion Classification on Imbalanced Data Using Deep Learning with Soft Attention
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying skin lesions on imbalanced data by proposing a deep learning framework that combines backbone models with Soft-Attention mechanisms and a custom loss function. The authors utilized the HAM10000 dataset, incorporating both dermoscopic images and patient metadata such as age and gender. Experimental results demonstrated that InceptionResNetV2 with Soft-Attention achieved 90% accuracy, with mean precision, F1-score, recall, and AUC values of 0.81, 0.81, 0.82, and 0.99, respectively. Additionally, the MobileNetV3Large model was evaluated, achieving 86% accuracy while requiring significantly fewer parameters and offering faster diagnosis times compared to InceptionResNetV2. The research highlights the effectiveness of integrating attention mechanisms and imbalance-aware loss functions for improving diagnostic accuracy in dermatology.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 7 classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments. 327 samples.

One of the 7 classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments. 1099 samples.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (7 classes: AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete lesion classes (e.g., melanoma vs. nevus vs. benign keratosis) using deep learning backbones (InceptionResNetV2, MobileNetV3Large) combined with Soft-Attention and metadata. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, F1-score, recall, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesion images published by Harvard University Dataverse, containing dermoscopic images and patient metadata (age, gender, localization).

use.training: 90% of the data was used for training the deep learning models.
use.validation: 10% of the data was used for validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate True Positives, False Positives, False Negatives, and True Negatives for each class to derive performance metrics.
To address class imbalance by assigning weights inversely proportional to the number of samples in each class, thereby improving recall for minority classes.
To increase the size of the training dataset and mitigate overfitting by applying rotation, shifting, zooming, and flipping to images.
To prevent overfitting by stopping training when validation accuracy does not increase for 25 epochs.
To minimize the loss function during model training with a learning rate of 0.001, reduced by a factor of 0.2.
To handle missing metadata values by randomly encoding properties as missing with a probability of p = 0.1 during training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, F1-score, recall, and AUC without statistical significance testing.

InceptionResNetV2.notes: Trained with metadata and new weighted loss function. Outperformed other models on accuracy, precision, F1-score, recall, and AUC. Confusion analysis showed difficulty distinguishing melanoma (MEL) and nevus (NV) due to similar colors.

DenseNet201.notes: Trained with metadata and new weighted loss function. Depth of 402 layers. Recall for AKIEC class improved to 0.85 compared to 0.65 without weighted loss.

MobileNetV3Large.notes: Trained with metadata and new weighted loss function. Parameters are 10 times less than InceptionResNetV2. Inference time is 30 times faster than InceptionResNetV2. Best performance on class DF with recall of 1.0.

ResNet50.notes: Trained with metadata. Accuracy was lower than ResNet50 trained with augmented data (0.76).

ResNet152.notes: Trained with metadata. Worst AUC performance among compared models.

NasNetMobile.notes: Trained with metadata. Best performance on class DF with recall of 0.92.

Soumyyak_et_al_[1].notes: Higher accuracy but lower F1-score and recall due to bias towards majority classes NV and VASC.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple backbone models combined with Soft-Attention and metadata inputs: InceptionResNetV2, MobileNetV3Large, DenseNet201, ResNet50, ResNet152, NasNetLarge, MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3Small, and NasNetMobile.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on ImageNet (implied by normalization requirements for DenseNet201, ResNet50/152, InceptionResNetV2, MobileNets, NasNet).

input_size.image: Original shape (450, 600) RGB, resized to model-specific requirements (e.g., 299x299 for InceptionResNetV2, 224x224 for ResNet/DenseNet/MobileNet variants).

architecture_details.backbone_modifications: Soft-Attention modules replaced specific layers in backbones: last three layers (DenseBlock, GAP, FC) for DenseNet201; last three layers (Residual Block, GAP, FC) for ResNet50/152; average pool and last dropout for InceptionResNetV2; last normal cell for NasNetLarge; last two 1x1 convolution layers for MobileNet versions; last normal cell for NasNetMobile.
architecture_details.metadata_branch: Metadata processed via a dense layer with 4096 neurons, concatenated with Soft-Attention output.
architecture_details.fusion: Concatenation of CNN feature vector (after global average pooling) and metadata branch output.
architecture_details.loss_function: New weighted loss function addressing class imbalance (weights inversely proportional to class sample counts).

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation, shifting, zooming, flipping (increased training set to 18,015 images).
training_details.missing_value_handling: Randomly encode metadata properties as missing with probability p=0.1 during training.
training_details.early_stopping: Stopped if validation accuracy did not increase for 25 epochs.

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to model-specific input sizes (e.g., 224x224 for DenseNet201, ResNet152, InceptionResNetV2; 229x229 for InceptionV3; 299x299 for InceptionResNetV2 as per architecture details; original shape 450x600 RGB)
Data augmentation including rotation, shifting, zooming, and flipping
Metadata normalization: Age numerically normalized; Gender and Localization encoded (one-hot or numerical encoding)
Handling missing metadata values by randomly encoding properties as missing with a probability of p = 0.1 during training

Image binarization with a very low threshold to find the center of mass (referenced in related work context for other methods, but not explicitly applied in the proposed method's pipeline description)
Padding images from (450, 600) to (600, 600) (referenced in related work context for WonderM, not the proposed method)
Resizing images to (448, 448) (referenced in related work context for SkinLinkNet, not the proposed method)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, shifting, zooming, and flipping were applied to images during training. This increased the training dataset size from 9,013 to 18,015 images.
A strategy to handle missing metadata values by randomly encoding each property (age, gender, localization) as missing with a probability of p = 0.1 during training.

conclusions: The proposed method combining InceptionResNetV2 with Soft-Attention mechanisms, patient metadata (age and gender), and a new weighted loss function achieved the highest performance with 90% accuracy, 0.81 precision, 0.81 F1-score, 0.82 recall, and 0.99 AUC on the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset.

The weighted loss function effectively addressed data imbalance, significantly improving recall for minority classes (e.g., AKIEC recall increased from 0.37 to 0.82 for InceptionResNetV2) compared to models trained without it or with standard data augmentation alone.

MobileNetV3Large combined with Soft-Attention and metadata achieved 86% accuracy with significantly fewer parameters (5.5 million, 10 times less than InceptionResNetV2) and depth, while offering 30 times faster inference time, making it suitable for mobile and IoT devices.

Models trained with metadata and the weighted loss function demonstrated more balanced performance across all classes, including low-sample classes, whereas models relying solely on data augmentation remained biased toward majority classes.

A key limitation acknowledged by the authors is the difficulty in distinguishing between melanoma and black nevi due to similar lesion sizes and colors, which remains a challenge for the model despite high overall metrics.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset without external validation on independent cohorts, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The dataset is heavily imbalanced, with Melanocytic nevi (NV) comprising 67.1% of samples (6705/10015) while Dermatofibroma (DF) has only 115 samples, necessitating the use of a custom weighted loss function.

Ground truth labels for the HAM10000 dataset are heterogeneous: only ~50% are confirmed via histopathology, while the remainder rely on follow-up, expert consensus, or in vivo confocal microscopy, introducing potential label noise.

The model exhibits a specific limitation in distinguishing between melanoma (MEL) and melanocytic nevi (NV) due to similar lesion colors and sizes, leading to misclassification of black nevi as melanoma.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons between models.

Data leakage risk exists as the HAM10000 dataset contains multiple images from the same patients; the paper does not explicitly confirm that patient-level splitting was used to prevent data leakage between training and validation sets.

The proposed method uses patient metadata (age, gender, localization) which may not be available in all clinical settings, potentially limiting deployment in resource-constrained environments where metadata is missing.
The study acknowledges that the HAM10000 dataset is biased towards certain demographics and lesion types, which may affect model performance on underrepresented groups.
